headlines.title: Public invited to weigh in on cellphone service quality during CPUC hearings
headlines.description: The California Public Utilities Commission is inviting the public to provide feedback on cellphone service quality during a series of upcoming hearings. With an estimated 29.5 million cellphone users in California, state regulators want to know how their service is holding up. The CPUC is set to hold four virtual public hearings. Two will take place on July 15, followed by two more on July 23. Any state wireless user can log in and listen, and anyone can call in to share their comments and feedback. According to The Utility Reform Network (TURN), the public hearings allow regulators to have direct information from rate payers. It’s also important for regulators to hear about reliability of service concerns, especially during emergencies. NBC Bay Area’s Sergio Quintana has more in the video report above.

headlines.title: SFO looking to build a private terminal for commercial flights
headlines.description: San Francisco International Airport is looking into a plan to create a private terminal for commercial flights. Travelers who are willing to pay could bypass the public terminals and get to their gate through a unique experience. Benefits would include privacy, security, efficiency, speed and comfort. This type of security check already exists at a few airports, including LAX. SFO is looking for operators and bidders to build and run the terminal. The potential facility would be located on the opposite side of the airport. SFO has been holding informational conferences for prospective bidders, with proposals due in October. The airport hopes to review proposals and award a successful bidder in December, with the potential facility opening by late 2028.

headlines.title: SF Fire Commission considering $6 million settlement in firefighter retaliation suit
headlines.description: The San Francisco Fire Commission met in closed session Wednesday to consider a $6 million legal settlement with a former firefighter who accused top department brass of retaliating against him for pursuing criminal charges against a fellow firefighter in an off-duty attack. The commission did not announce any action after its closed-door discussion, officials said. If the commission votes to accept, the deal must still be approved by the city’s Board of Supervisors before it becomes official. The City Attorney’s spokeswoman, Jen Kwart, said the office believes the proposed settlement “is an appropriate resolution given the inherent costs of litigation.” In the suit, former firefighter Gabriel Shin names then Chief Jeanine Nicholson and other SFFD officials as having retaliated against him after an incident Feb. 1, 2022, involving fellow firefighter, Robert Muhammad. According to Oakland police, Muhammad attacked Shin outside Shin’s home in Oakland. Shin suffered a broken arm and a concussion after Muhammad allegedly struck him with a firefighting spanner wrench, according to Shin’s 2023 lawsuit. While Shin was suspended without pay after the attack and ultimately left the department, the suit alleges, Muhammad was allowed to remain with the department for three years after the attack. Muhammad ultimately received mental health diversion in the criminal assault case. The settlement deal came after U.S. District Judge Vince Chhabria rejected the city’s attempt to dismiss the suit. In his ruling in February, Chhabria found “plenty of evidence” suggesting fire department officials “conspired to harass, pressure, and punish Shin over his pursuing criminal charges against Muhammad.” The judge found it “difficult to understand how these officials could have thought their conduct in protecting Muhammad and disfavoring Shin was justified. It’s even more difficult to imagine a jury reaching that conclusion.’’ Judge Chhabria also found evidence to back Shin’s contentions that the department failed to heed Shin’s warning that Muhammad had threatened him beforehand. According to the suit, Muhammad looked up Shin’s address in the department’s personnel database and took a 15-inch-long brass spanner wrench to Shin’s home. The suit alleges Shin was sweeping the sidewalk with neighbors when Muhammad came up from behind and confronted him. “I’m going to give you a chance to save yourself,” Muhammad told Shin, the suit alleges. When Shin put the broom down and refused to fight, the suit alleges the taller, younger Muhammad swung the spanner at his head. According to the suit, Shin broke his arm while fending off the attack that left him with a concussion and other injuries. His suit alleges that an armed bystander intervened to stop the attack. The suit alleges that afterwards, the department refused to cooperate with Oakland police investigators who sought Muhammad’s photo to prepare an arrest warrant. “Without that information from the SFFD, the police department could not quickly identify Defendant Muhammad, including because of the number of people who share the same name,” the suit alleges. The suit alleges that Shin was subjected to a pressure campaign to get him to refuse to cooperate with the investigation. A supervisor contacted him a day after the attack, asking if “we could work something out” in lieu of criminal charges. After Shin refused, the suit alleges another supervisor issued a “direct order to end his cooperation” with the Oakland police probe. When he went to the chief, the suit states she “did not offer to take any corrective action to stop SFFD witness and victim intimidation.” “When firefighter Shin resisted the hostile pressure and direct order from SFFD superior officers to cease cooperation with the criminal investigation against defendant Muhammad,” according to the suit, “Chief Nicholson punished Firefighter Shin by stripping him of his compensation and taking away his health insurance before the broken bones in his arm had even set.” Although Muhammad was initially sent home without pay, the department changed that stance after the head of the Black Firefighters Union objected and claimed that such an action was unprecedented.

headlines.title: San Jose may be home to a new data center
headlines.description: A new data center may be coming to South San Jose. A 500,000-square-foot data center is being planned by Prologis and would be located on Silver Creek Valley Road. The building is proposed to be three stories high with 30,000 square feet of office space. There is no update on when construction will start or when the center will be open. Prologis said it will make sure that construction will have a minimal impact on the community.

headlines.title: Group urges changes to how Congress handles sexual harassment complaints
headlines.description: Following Rep. Eric Swalwell’s resignation, a group representing more than 1,500 congressional staffers called for sweeping changes to how Congress handles sexual harassment and misconduct complaints. Congressional staffers say Congress is failing to protect staff from sexual abuse and misconduct. In a letter to house and senate leaders, the Congressional Progressive Staff Association, which represents more than 1,500 House and Senate employees, is urging leaders to change how harassment complaints are handled on Capitol Hill. Sonoma State Political Science Professor David Mccuan says the proposals target some of the biggest barriers to reporting harassment. “This moment is different,” he said. “The last really big congressional staffers piece of legation that passed was in 1995.” The letter follows recent misconduct allegations involving East Bay Congressman Eric Swalwell, who has denied the claims, as well as the resignation of former Texas Congressman Tony Gonzales after admitting to an affair with a staffer who later died by suicide. The group is calling for independent investigations, expanded legal support for staff, more funding for the office of congressional workplace rights, and better education about how to report misconduct. “One of the changes that the group wants to talk about is the changes is the statue of limitations which is currently 180 days. Congress doesn’t meet that often and its often gone or out of session and if you are a victim of some type of assault or harassment you may be uncertain as to what to do,” McCuan said. The letter also calls for extending that reporting deadline to at least one year, saying the current timeline forces some employees to decide whether to take legal action before they’re ready. The group says recent misconduct allegations have exposed major flaws in the current system. The staff association says, “congressional staff should never have to choose between their safety and their careers in public service.” “If implemented, they would do a few things,” McCuan said. “They would take things we see in the private sector, at a university or many public sectors that protect workers and protect their rights and put together a process that makes more sense. That could improve the environment and that could go a long way in terms of changing the tenor or tone. The association says the law reforms would create a fairer process for staff while helping Congress retain employees at a time of record staff turnover. The group is calling for ranking members to take this up before the august recess and if not the beginning of the next Congress in January.

headlines.title: American Century Championship: Annual celebrity golf event returns to Lake Tahoe
headlines.description: World-famous athletes and celebrities are descending on Tahoe this week for the 2026 American Century Championship golf tournament. The three-day, 54-hole competition at Edgewood Tahoe Golf Course near South Lake Tahoe tees off Friday and concludes on Sunday. A couple of Bay Area sports legends have won the tournament in recent years. Former San Jose Sharks star Joe Pavelski is the reigning champion, while Golden State Warriors star Steph Curry hoisted the trophy in 2023. NBC Bay Area’s Anthony Flores provides a preview of this year’s event in the video player above. golf 7 hours ago Steph Curry May 15 Steph Curry shares ambitious golf plans after his legendary NBA career is over

headlines.title: When is the 2026 American Century Championship? How to watch, participants, more
headlines.description: Celebrity golfers are returning to Nevada for the latest edition of the American Century Championship. The 2026 edition will feature 80 celebrities across sports and entertainment competing in a three-day, 54-hole tournament. The 37th year of the championship also raises money for local and national charities. So, where will the action be available to watch and who will be participating? Here’s everything to know: golf May 18, 2025 Masters Tournament Apr 10, 2025 When is the 2026 American Century Championship? The championship runs from Wednesday, July 8 to Sunday, July 12. Where is the 2026 American Century Championship? Edgewood Tahoe Golf Course in Nevada, on the shore of Lake Tahoe, is the venue. Who is competing in the 2026 American Century Championship? Popular names include former NHL player and reigning champion Joe Pavelski, Golden State Warriors star Stephen Curry, NBA legend Charles Barkley, “Saturday Night Live’s” Colin Jost, WNBA commissioner Cathy Engelbert and more. See the full list here. What is the purse for the 2026 American Century Championship? The total purse is $750,000. The winner will claim $150,000, second place collects $70,000, and third place $40,000. How to watch the 2026 American Century Championship on TV, online Here’s how to watch the action (all times listed in EST): Friday, July 10: 3-6 p.m.- Peacock and NBCSN Saturday, July 11: 2:30 p.m. – 6 p.m. – NBC and Peacock Sunday, July 12: 2:30 p.m. – 6 p.m. – NBC and Peacock 2026 American Century Championship schedule Wednesday and Thursday will feature practice rounds before the real action commences Friday. Here’s the breakdown: Friday, July 10 7:45 a.m. ET — American Century Championship First Round Saturday, July 11 7:45 a.m. ET — American Century Championship Second Round Sunday, July 12 7:45 a.m. ET — American Century Championship Final Round 3:00 p.m. ET — Winner’s Trophy Presentation, 18 Green

headlines.title: San Jose family mourns grandfather who died after motorcycle crash
headlines.description: A family in San Jose is mourning the loss of a grandfather who died following a motorcycle crash last month. James Villegas, 64, was riding his motorcycle on June 22 when he collided with a pickup truck on North 10th Street. He was rushed to the hospital after the crash and died a week later. Maryann Villegas said losing her husband has been devastating. “All I know is he went for a bike ride and I get a phone call from the doctor asking me if James Villegas is my husband and what his injuries were and he was in critical condition,” she said. Villegas’ loved ones are organizing a car wash and food sale to raise money for his funeral. The event will be held at 10 a.m. on Saturday and Sunday at Hillview Park in San Jose.

headlines.title: San Jose police seek suspects who stole statue from Vietnamese Heritage Garden
headlines.description: San Jose police are asking for the public’s help in identifying two people who stole a large statue from the Vietnamese Heritage Garden last month. The roughly 9-foot statue of a South Vietnamese soldier was stolen from the garden, located in Kelley Park, on June 21 and later recovered along a creek bed. The South Vietnamese soldier statue stands next to an American soldier statue as part of a monument called “Thank You America,” a gesture to the American soldiers who fought with the South Vietnamese during the Vietnam War. Citing a preliminary investigation, police said the statue was stolen sometime between 4 and 4:30 a.m. on June 21. At about 3:30 p.m. the same day, officers found the statue near the Coyote Creek Trail and Interstate 280. During the course of the investigation, officers also found a stolen commercial truck they believe was used during the theft. The truck was reported stolen on June 18 from a business along the 1900 block of Monterey Road, police said. The two suspects have not been identified and remain outstanding. Anyone who recognizes the suspects, witnessed the theft or has information about it is encouraged to contact San Jose police Detective Cobb by email at 4812@sanjoseca.gov or by phone at 408-277-4521. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Monthly San Francisco Chinatown Night Market adds free Mahjong play
headlines.description: Summer in San Francisco means festivals and night markets almost every weekend, with the goal of attracting tourists to different neighborhoods. Every second Friday of the month through October, the BeChinatown Night Market will take place with the added addition of “Mahjong on Waverly.” The tile-based game originated in China in the 19th century and has exploded in popularity worldwide. NBC Bay Area’s Janelle Wang got some Mahjong tips from Jaimie Lau of Mo Pai Mahjong Club. See more in the video player above.

headlines.title: Sam Altman arson suspect remains in custody as mental health report filed
headlines.description: The man accused of targeting OpenAI CEO Sam Altman’s San Francisco home and the company’s headquarters appeared in court Wednesday for what attorneys described as a status hearing. Daniel Moreno Gama, 20, of Texas, remains in custody facing federal and state charges, including attempted murder and arson, in connection with an April incident. During Wednesday’s hearing, a mental health evaluation report found Moreno Gama eligible for mental health diversion. However, his defense attorney said the defense is not pursuing that option at this time. “Daniel needs care and not to be caged,” Deputy Public Defender Diamond Ward said. “He is a young, 20-year-old who got lost. And I think the DA’s false narrative and oversimplification of the facts of this case is going to come to light.” According to prosecutors, surveillance images show Moreno Gama throwing an explosive device at the gate of Altman’s San Francisco home during the early morning hours of April 10. Prosecutors also allege he later went to OpenAI headquarters in San Francisco, where he attempted to damage the property. San Francisco May 26 San Francisco May 5 Man accused of attacking OpenAI CEO Sam Altman's home pleads not guilty to attempted murder

headlines.title: ‘It's hard to be here, but we are here': SF Black Wall Street expands its mission
headlines.description: It’s no secret the Black population is shrinking in the Bay Area. Latest Census figures say it’s at 6% in the entire region. Despite those declines, one nonprofit wants to expand in hopes of preserving Black culture and businesses. “It’s hard to be here, but we are here,” said Tinisch Hollins, who has organized in her community for decades. The San Francisco native grew up in the Bayview and now lives in Solano County. “I mean the economic wealth gap. I don’t live in San Francisco anymore because I can’t afford to buy a home in San Francisco,” she said. Hollins co-founded SF Black Wall Street in 2020 during the pandemic to bring more visibility to Black-owned businesses and culture. In February 2025, SF Black Wall Street opened 1921 Lounge in the Bayview. The lounge is named after the year the original Black Wall Street in Tulsa, Oklahoma, was destroyed by race riots. “Our goal is to make our first social enterprise for our organization so that we can raise our own capital,” Hollins said. 1921 Lounge was damaged in June 2025 and was forced to shut down. Hollins is now working through the permit process to reopen. “Our Black population, no secret, has declined,” Hollins said. “We’re the minority across the Bay Area, and it’s important for us to build visibility and to build connection across the Bay Area so that our culture and businesses stay alive.” “I’m not giving up on my city. That’s home base,” she went on say. “But I recognize that in order for us to anchor our Black communities, we have to go beyond San Francisco and anchor all of them.” Building those connections means expanding the community network. SF Black Wall Street is now known as Bay Area Black Wall Street. The newly renovated Fedora Lounge and Comedy Club in Suisun City will be part of Bay Area Black Wall Street’s growing network to build culture and community. Tegre Miles owns Fedora. “We can sit back and go with the narrative that people, other people write for us, or we can do it ourselves,” he said. Bay Area Black Wall Street wants its members to have meeting places to connect and celebrate but also organize and strategize community needs. Solano County Supervisor Cassandra James was raised in San Francisco and now lives in Vallejo. “When we’re talking about Black people in the Bay Area, Solano County has the most Black residents per capita than any other county in the Bay Area,” she said. Solano County has the highest Black population at 13%. Alameda County is at 9%, Contra Costa is 8% and San Francisco is at 5%. The Black populations in Marin, Santa Clara, Sonoma and Napa counties are at 1-2%. Hollins said investment is important for significant change. It’s also necessary for narratives to change. “I feel like the Black population is sometimes seen and not welcome, not seen for the right reasons, and I think that’s what needs to shift,” she said. “It’s something you need to invest in, embrace, and really make that whole diversity thing that folks like to say, make it a real thing cause Black culture is a driving force.”
headlines.source: Christine Ni, Jeremy Carroll and Marcus Washington
